source.title: Spatial representations of self and other in the hippocampus

confidence_rationale: Replicates the social-place-cell finding in rats with a different paradigm (observational T-maze) and identifies CA1 cells that jointly encode self and other. Companion to Omer 2018 in bats — together they establish social position as a cross-species privileged map channel.
failure_signatures: Rat dorsal CA1 contains cells that jointly and discretely encode the spatial location of self and another rat; some cells have identical receptive fields for self and other.
If REE assumed each agent has its own self-only spatial map, this falsifies that assumption: the same map natively represents another agent's trajectory.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Dorsal CA1 pyramidal cells in observer rats jointly and discretely encode the spatial location of both self and another rat in an observational T-maze; a subset have identical receptive fields for self and other.
mapping.ree_translation: The shared-receptive-field result is the strongest version of the social-channel claim: the same place-coding machinery is used for self-position and conspecific-position, with some cells genuinely abstract over the agent identity. This argues that 'agent in space' may be a more fundamental representational primitive than 'self in space.'
mapping.mapping_caveat: Observational T-maze is a constrained paradigm; the rats are restrained and observe a fixed trajectory. Whether the same cells generalize to multiple conspecifics or to free social interaction is not addressed.
mapping.source_context: Long-Evans rats, observational T-maze task, single-unit recording in dorsal CA1 of observer rat during demonstrator runs.

confidence_components.notes: Science paper, companion to Omer 2018 in bats. Rat result reduces transfer risk for the cross-species claim. Confidence is bounded by the constrained T-maze paradigm.
